title: Nation Marks Coup Anniversary as Özel Challenges Kılıçdaroğlu, Istanbul Court Targets Netanyahu
subhead: Türkiye commemorates the 10th anniversary of the July 15 failed coup with nationwide ceremonies, while opposition leader Özgür Özel publicly challenges reinstated chairman Kemal Kılıçdaroğlu to an in-party vote. An Istanbul court issued an international arrest request for Benjamin Netanyahu over the Gaza flotilla, and the Ahbap charity corruption probe widened with 17 more detentions.

sections.heading: Türkiye Marks 10th Anniversary of July 15 Failed Coup
sections.body:
Türkiye commemorated the 10th anniversary of the failed July 15, 2016 coup attempt with nationwide ceremonies under the theme 'Our Will, Our Victory.' The putsch, orchestrated by what Ankara calls the Fethullah Gulen Terrorist Organization (FETO), killed 253 people and left more than 2,700 injured when a military faction deployed tanks and fighter jets against civilians and state institutions, including parliament.

President Recep Tayyip Erdoğan attended a commemorative ceremony at parliament before heading to a separate evening event in Ankara expected to draw around 20,000 participants at a public garden under presidential auspices. In Istanbul, thousands gathered for a memorial march across the July 15 Martyrs Bridge — formerly the Bosphorus Bridge, where 34 civilians were killed by coup soldiers' fire — with organizers expecting around 5,000 attendees. A synchronized 1,500-drone light show over the Bosphorus, a digital time tunnel in Ankara's Kızılay district, and an interactive 'Memory Tree' installation in Istanbul's Üsküdar blended commemoration with digital immersion for younger generations. Museums in 54 provinces hosted exhibitions and panel discussions welcoming coup resistance veterans.

The anniversary comes one day after coordinated operations targeting 968 FETO suspects across all 81 provinces, which the Interior and Justice ministries described as part of a 'major cleansing effort across all institutions.' FETO founder Fethullah Gülen died in October 2024 at age 83 in Pennsylvania, where he had lived for more than two decades.
sections.whyItMatters: A decade on, the July 15 anniversary remains a defining frame for the Turkish state — simultaneously a moment of national unity and an instrument of political consolidation. The scale of the preceding day's 968-person FETO sweep underscores how the anniversary doubles as an enforcement mechanism for the security apparatus.

sections.heading: Özel Challenges Kılıçdaroğlu to In-Party CHP Leadership Vote
sections.body:
Özgür Özel, the ousted leader of the main opposition Republican People's Party (CHP), publicly challenged reinstated chairman Kemal Kılıçdaroğlu to an in-party leadership election, escalating the party's internal crisis. Speaking at the weekly CHP parliamentary group meeting on July 14, Özel declared: 'I am making an open call from this podium: Let's go to an election with 2 million members of the CHP and the defeated will leave politics. I announce that I will quit politics if my votes remain under 90 percent.'

Özel was removed from the chairmanship after an Ankara court annulled the 2023 CHP congress over fraud allegations, reinstating Kılıçdaroğlu as interim leader. Özel criticized the court for not yet submitting its ruling to the Court of Appeals, saying: 'It's been 54 days since they have given this unlawful decision. And as they know it will be turned down by the Court of Appeals, they are trying to prolong the process until July 20, the day the 40-day judicial recess starts.'

Özel also expressed strong support for Çankaya Mayor Hüseyin Can Güner, who was detained on corruption allegations, claiming the government-led crackdown on the opposition has spread to Ankara after targeting Istanbul municipalities. 'Until now, 34 CHP municipalities have faced judicial proceedings with the mayors of 24 of them, mostly from Istanbul, arrested,' Özel stated.
sections.whyItMatters: Özel's challenge is an attempt to break the legal deadlock by forcing a democratic contest within the party rather than waiting for the appeals court. If Kılıçdaroğlu refuses, Özel gains the moral high ground; if he accepts, the party gets clarity — but the deeper issue remains the judicial intervention that removed Özel in the first place, which the government has not disavowed.

sections.heading: Istanbul Court Issues International Arrest Request for Netanyahu Over Gaza Flotilla
sections.body:
The Istanbul 11th High Criminal Court ordered the issuance of an international arrest request for Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu over Israel's interception of a Gaza-bound humanitarian aid flotilla, Hürriyet Daily News reports. The decision came during the ongoing trial concerning the Israeli military's seizure of the Sumud aid flotilla, which included Turkish nationals among its passengers.

Prosecutors accuse Netanyahu and several senior Israeli officials of crimes including crimes against humanity, genocide, intentional injury, and torture. The indictment also alleged property damage, aggravated looting, obstruction of transportation, hijacking or unlawful detention of vessels, and unlawful deprivation of liberty.

The proceedings follow an earlier ruling in November 2025, when an Istanbul court issued preliminary arrest warrants for Netanyahu and dozens of other Israeli officials over both the flotilla incident and alleged crimes during Israel's military campaign in Gaza. In April 2026, the Istanbul Chief Public Prosecutor's Office completed an indictment seeking aggravated life sentences for the defendants, arguing that Turkish courts have jurisdiction under international maritime law.
sections.whyItMatters: The warrant is largely symbolic — Netanyahu will not be arrested on Turkish soil — but it is a deliberate escalation in Türkiye's legal and diplomatic posture toward Israel. It reinforces Ankara's role as a legal advocate for the Palestinian cause and keeps the Gaza flotilla issue alive in international discourse at a time when the broader regional focus has shifted to the US-Iran confrontation.

sections.heading: Ahbap Charity Probe Widens: 17 More Detained as Financial Trail Deepens
sections.body:
Prosecutors expanded the corruption and money laundering investigation into the Ahbap Association, the prominent charity founded by singer Haluk Levent, ordering the detention of 17 more suspects in a second wave of operations. Raids were carried out simultaneously in Istanbul, İzmir, Kocaeli, and Muğla, targeting 14 suspects and four companies. Three of those facing detention orders were already in prison on unrelated charges, bringing the total number of suspects detained in the case to 39.

Levent himself remains in custody among the initial 22 detainees, facing allegations of misuse of association funds, money laundering, and membership in a criminal organization. Prosecutors linked the latest operation to an allegedly irregular contract awarded for a festival, with Levent's brother Berkant Acil securing the contract through a production company acting as a front while expenses for stage, lighting, and sound equipment were allegedly inflated using fraudulent invoices.

Authorities allege that part of donations to Ahbap was transferred to accounts belonging to Levent's assistant before approximately 125.8 million Turkish Liras ($2.7 million) was moved to Acil and associated individuals and companies. A separate MASAK (Financial Crimes Investigation Board) report identified unusually large banking transactions involving several suspects, with account activity reaching hundreds of millions and, in one case, more than 1.4 billion liras between 2020 and 2024.
sections.whyItMatters: Ahbap gained nationwide recognition for coordinating donations after the devastating 2023 earthquakes that killed over 53,000 people. The widening probe — now with 39 detainees and a 1.4 billion lira financial trail — risks destroying public trust in one of Türkiye's most prominent civilian disaster-response organizations. Whether the investigation is proportionate or politically motivated is a question the prosecution's trajectory will answer.

sections.heading: Hormuz Squeeze Deepens: Six Tankers Pass as US Strikes Iran for Third Day
sections.body:
The Strait of Hormuz crisis intensified as US forces struck Iran for a third consecutive day on July 14, with President Donald Trump vowing to reimpose a naval blockade and Iran's Revolutionary Guard Corps firing missiles and drones at Bahrain, targeting a residential building for US forces and other facilities. Bahrain said it intercepted 'several treacherous aerial attacks launched by Iran' and accused Tehran of targeting civilians.

Hormuz traffic has slowed dramatically: according to Kpler data, only six oil tankers passed through the strait on July 12, and LNG carriers made no transits over the weekend. Brent crude rose nearly 5% on July 13, approaching $80 per barrel, and gained an additional 1% on July 14. Iran's parliament has begun work on a bill on 'the management of the Strait of Hormuz,' while Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi mocked Trump's proposed 20% cargo levy, suggesting Tehran would charge less.

For Türkiye, the impact is indirect but material. According to Central Bank calculations cited by Hürriyet Daily News, every permanent $10 increase in oil prices expands the current account deficit by approximately $2.6 billion over 12 months. Türkiye's energy import bill rose 43.4% year-on-year in May to $6.11 billion, per Anadolu Agency data. If oil settles at $100 instead of $80, a roughly $5 billion annual gap could open in the current account; at $120, the burden could approach $10 billion. The Central Bank estimates a permanent 10% oil price shock adds roughly one percentage point to consumer inflation by year-end.
sections.whyItMatters: Türkiye doesn't buy oil through Hormuz, but it cannot escape the prices shaped there. With Brent approaching $80 and only six tankers passing on July 12, the squeeze is no longer hypothetical — it is already feeding into the current account deficit and inflation outlook. The Marmaraereğlisi LNG terminal expansion announced this week (adding 160,000 m³ of storage) is a hedge, but it won't offset the price transmission mechanism.

sections.heading: Iraq Pipeline Compensation Heads to Washington as Türkiye Expands LNG Capacity
sections.body:
Energy Minister Alparslan Bayraktar said a separate enforcement case in Washington will calculate mutual claims and accrued interest before a final balance is determined on the Iraq-Türkiye pipeline compensation, following the Paris Court of Appeal's upholding of a $1.471 billion ruling against Türkiye. 'We will see who will ultimately be the creditor after those calculations are completed. A final decision has not yet emerged,' Bayraktar told reporters on July 14.

The arbitration tribunal ruled in February 2023 that Türkiye should pay Iraq net compensation for transporting and selling Iraqi oil through the pipeline and loading it at the Ceyhan terminal without Baghdad's approval between May 2014 and September 2018. Türkiye's appeal seeking annulment was rejected by the Paris court.

Separately, Bayraktar announced that the Marmaraereğlisi LNG Terminal in Tekirdağ will add a fourth storage tank of 160,000 cubic meters, increasing total capacity from 255,000 to 415,000 cubic meters. The terminal handles an average of 65 LNG tankers annually and meets approximately 15% of Türkiye's yearly natural gas demand. Bayraktar said the investment would 'strengthen the country's natural gas infrastructure and improve resilience against potential crises in global energy markets.' Türkiye currently has five LNG entry points — two terminals and three Floating Storage and Regasification Units — with regasification capacity nearly fivefold since 2016 to 161 million cubic meters per day. The ministry aims to raise this to 200 million cubic meters per day with two new FSRUs.
sections.whyItMatters: The Iraq pipeline dispute is heading toward a Washington resolution that could adjust the $1.471 billion figure, but Bayraktar's insistence that the final creditor remains undetermined signals Türkiye is not accepting the Paris ruling as final. Meanwhile, the LNG expansion is a direct response to Hormuz vulnerability — Türkiye is methodically building import flexibility, even as the export-side pipeline dispute drags on.

sections.heading: AKP Proposes Life Sentences for Minors in Homicide Cases
sections.body:
The ruling AKP submitted an 18-article bill that would allow courts to impose life or aggravated life imprisonment on offenders aged 15 to 18 convicted of intentional murder, the most contentious change to Türkiye's juvenile justice framework in years. The proposal, announced by AKP Deputy Parliamentary Group Chair Leyla Şahin Usta, was prompted by public outrage over the killing of 15-year-old Mattia Ahmet Minguzzi in Istanbul, who was stabbed in a market by two suspects both under 18.

Under the draft, judges would gain discretion to reduce or eliminate sentence reductions currently applicable to minors in particularly serious cases, based on factors including intent and prior convictions. The minimum age for repeat-offender provisions would drop from 18 to 15, and for children aged 12 to 15, judges would gain greater discretion to impose harsher penalties in serious violent crimes.

Other provisions would require juvenile offenders to begin serving prison terms in closed juvenile correctional facilities before transfer to educational institutions for good behavior. The bill would end the rule counting one day served before age 15 as two days when calculating parole eligibility in crimes such as murder, sexual offences, drug trafficking, and organized crime. It would also introduce one to three years imprisonment for firearm owners whose negligent storage allows children to access weapons, and increase penalties for parents whose failure to fulfill family obligations contributes to a child's involvement in serious violent crime. The government aims to secure parliamentary approval before the summer recess.
sections.whyItMatters: The Minguzzi case tapped into deep public frustration, but the bill's scope extends well beyond the triggering incident — effectively rewriting Türkiye's juvenile sentencing framework. Child rights advocates have criticized similar proposals in the past for undermining rehabilitation in favor of retribution, and the timing before the summer recess leaves little room for substantive parliamentary debate.

bottomLine: July 15 is Türkiye's most solemn domestic anniversary, and this year's 10th commemoration carried extra weight after Monday's 968-person FETO sweep. But the day's other developments will likely outlast the ceremonies: Özel's challenge to Kılıçdaroğlu forces a choice between a democratic contest and indefinite legal paralysis in the CHP, while the Istanbul court's Netanyahu warrant signals that Ankara has no intention of letting its legal campaign against Israel fade. The Ahbap probe — now at 39 detainees with a 1.4 billion lira financial trail — is becoming a test of whether a disaster-response charity can survive a money-laundering investigation of this scale. Meanwhile, Hormuz is squeezing harder: only six tankers passed on July 12, oil is approaching $80, and the Central Bank's own math says every $10 of permanent price increase adds $2.6 billion to the current account deficit and roughly one point to inflation. The LNG terminal expansion and the Iraq pipeline extension are hedges, but they do not change the price transmission mechanism.
sources: Hürriyet Daily News, Anadolu Agency, Kpler
